1:
"But the children of Israel committed a trespass in the accursed thing: for Achan... took of the accursed thing: and the anger of the LORD was kindled against the children of Israel." The witnesses note the trespass in the accursed thing — one man, Achan, took of the devoted spoil, yet God's anger burned against all Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was done not by the whole body but by one, yet, not being discovered, it was imputed to all. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Achan taking of the accursed thing, the anger of the LORD kindled. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a trespass in the accursed thing — the devoted spoil taken; the Achan took — one man's secret sin; the charged to all Israel — the body guilty; the the contagion of sin — a little leaven leavens the lump (1 Cor. 5:6).

The witnesses agree the verse opens the dark sequel to the triumph of Jericho — one man's secret sin bringing guilt on the whole nation: "the children of Israel committed a trespass," though "Achan... took of the accursed thing"; the sin of a single member is charged to the body, "the anger of the LORD... kindled against the children of Israel"; the corporate guilt teaches the solidarity of God's people in sin and the contagion of one man's transgression, "a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump" (1 Cor. 5:6); it warns that hidden sin in the camp defiles all and provokes God's anger against the whole.

So the witnesses make the verse the trespass: The witnesses note the a trespass in the accursed thing: the devoted spoil taken. The witnesses note the Achan took: one man's secret sin. The witnesses note the charged to all Israel: the body guilty. The witnesses note the the contagion of sin: a little leaven leavens the lump (1 Cor. 5:6). The expositors observe the a trespass in the accursed thing: the devoted spoil taken. The expositors observe the Achan took: one man's secret sin. The expositors observe the charged to all Israel: the body guilty. The expositors observe the the contagion of sin: a little leaven leavens the lump (1 Cor. 5:6). The believer is taught to mark the a trespass in the accursed thing, the Achan took, the charged to all Israel, the the contagion of sin, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that Israel trespassed in the accursed thing, Achan taking of the devoted spoil — one man's secret sin bringing guilt on all, a little leaven leavening the whole lump (1 Cor. 5:6).
2:
"And Joshua sent men from Jericho to Ai... and spake unto them, saying, Go up and view the country. And the men went up and viewed Ai." The witnesses note Joshua sending men to spy out Ai, the next city to be taken. The witnesses note that Gill notes Ai was the next city of importance, smaller than Jericho, a few miles distant. The witnesses note that the men went up and viewed it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sent men to Ai — the next city; the go up and view — ordinary prudence; the they viewed Ai — the plans laid; the vain without God — except the LORD keep the city (Ps. 127:1).

The witnesses agree the verse shows the ordinary prudence of war — Joshua sending spies to view Ai before the assault; nothing seems amiss, the leader proceeding as before with due care; yet, unknown to him, the camp lay under God's displeasure for Achan's sin, and so the careful plans would fail; it teaches that even wise and diligent measures cannot prosper while sin is unpurged, the favour of God being the true success of all our undertakings, "except the LORD keep the city, the watchman waketh but in vain" (Ps. 127:1), the spying of Ai undertaken under a hidden curse.

So the witnesses make the verse view the country: The witnesses note the sent men to Ai: the next city. The witnesses note the go up and view: ordinary prudence. The witnesses note the they viewed Ai: the plans laid. The witnesses note the vain without God: except the LORD keep the city (Ps. 127:1). The expositors observe the sent men to Ai: the next city. The expositors observe the go up and view: ordinary prudence. The expositors observe the they viewed Ai: the plans laid. The expositors observe the vain without God: except the LORD keep the city (Ps. 127:1). The believer is taught to mark the sent men to Ai, the go up and view, the they viewed Ai, the vain without God, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that Joshua sent men to view Ai — ordinary prudence, yet the camp under a hidden curse, the plans to fail, for except the LORD keep the city the watchman wakes in vain (Ps. 127:1).
3:
"And they returned unto Joshua, and said unto him, Let not all the people go up; but let about two or three thousand men go up and smite Ai..." The witnesses note the spies advising that only a few thousand need go up against the small city. The witnesses note that Gill notes after reconnoitring they returned and gave it as their opinion that the whole army need not go up. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the counsel to send two or three thousand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let not all go up — the small city underrated; the two or three thousand — self-confident counsel; the presuming on themselves — forgetting the victory was God's; the the danger of presumption — let him that standeth take heed (1 Cor. 10:12).

The witnesses agree the spies' counsel breathed a self-confidence that overlooked their dependence on God: judging Ai a small and easy prize, they advised that "not all the people" need go, but a few thousand would suffice; the underrating of the task and the trust in their own sufficiency, fresh from the victory of Jericho, betray a forgetting that the victory was God's, not theirs; it teaches the danger of presumption after success, that the people of God are never to lean on their own strength, "let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall" (1 Cor. 10:12), the easy victory presumed where God was not.

So the witnesses make the verse a few thousand: The witnesses note the let not all go up: the small city underrated. The witnesses note the two or three thousand: self-confident counsel. The witnesses note the presuming on themselves: forgetting the victory was God's. The witnesses note the the danger of presumption: let him that standeth take heed (1 Cor. 10:12). The expositors observe the let not all go up: the small city underrated. The expositors observe the two or three thousand: self-confident counsel. The expositors observe the presuming on themselves: forgetting the victory was God's. The expositors observe the the danger of presumption: let him that standeth take heed (1 Cor. 10:12). The believer is taught to mark the let not all go up, the two or three thousand, the presuming on themselves, the the danger of presumption, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that the spies advised only a few thousand against Ai — self-confidence overlooking dependence on God, the danger of presumption after success, let him that standeth take heed lest he fall (1 Cor. 10:12).
4:
"So there went up thither of the people about three thousand men: and they fled before the men of Ai." The witnesses note the three thousand going up, only to flee before the men of Ai. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua detached the largest number proposed, that there might be strength enough; they went to the very gate. The witnesses note that they fled before the men of Ai. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the three thousand went up — thought enough; the fled before Ai — routed by the weaker; the God not with them — the hidden cause; the defeat for sin — ye shall flee when none pursueth (Lev. 26:17).

The witnesses agree the unexpected rout exposed the hidden cause — God was not with them: the three thousand, thought enough, "fled before the men of Ai," routed by a far weaker foe; the shameful flight, where they looked for easy victory, was the rebuke of providence for the sin in the camp; it teaches that without God's presence the strongest fail before the weakest, and that defeat and disgrace befall his people when sin has driven away his favour, "ye shall flee when none pursueth you" (Lev. 26:17), the flight of Israel the token of a forsaken host.

So the witnesses make the verse they fled: The witnesses note the three thousand went up: thought enough. The witnesses note the fled before Ai: routed by the weaker. The witnesses note the God not with them: the hidden cause. The witnesses note the defeat for sin: ye shall flee when none pursueth (Lev. 26:17). The expositors observe the three thousand went up: thought enough. The expositors observe the fled before Ai: routed by the weaker. The expositors observe the God not with them: the hidden cause. The expositors observe the defeat for sin: ye shall flee when none pursueth (Lev. 26:17). The believer is taught to mark the three thousand went up, the fled before Ai, the God not with them, the defeat for sin, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that the three thousand fled before Ai — the rout exposing that God was not with them, defeat befalling his people when sin has driven away his favour, fleeing when none pursueth (Lev. 26:17).
5:
"And the men of Ai smote of them about thirty and six men... wherefore the hearts of the people melted, and became as water." The witnesses note thirty-six slain, and the hearts of the people melting like water. The witnesses note that Gill notes their loss was small, but their shame and disgrace great — a sufficient rebuke of providence. The witnesses note that Clarke notes they were chased from the gate even unto Shebarim. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smote thirty-six — the small loss; the great disgrace — a sufficient rebuke; the hearts melted — the courage broken; the unmanned in sin — the bold made as water (Prov. 28:1).

The witnesses agree the small loss carried a great rebuke and broke the people's courage: only thirty-six fell, yet "the hearts of the people melted, and became as water"; the very people whose advance had melted the hearts of their enemies now found their own hearts melting, the courage that should have been theirs given over to fear; Gill marks the loss small but the disgrace great; it teaches that when God withdraws, the boldest are unmanned, the same fear that should fall on the wicked seizing his people in their sin, "the wicked flee when no man pursueth: but the righteous are bold as a lion" (Prov. 28:1) — the courage of Israel now turned to water.

So the witnesses make the verse hearts melted: The witnesses note the smote thirty-six: the small loss. The witnesses note the great disgrace: a sufficient rebuke. The witnesses note the hearts melted: the courage broken. The witnesses note the unmanned in sin: the bold made as water (Prov. 28:1). The expositors observe the smote thirty-six: the small loss. The expositors observe the great disgrace: a sufficient rebuke. The expositors observe the hearts melted: the courage broken. The expositors observe the unmanned in sin: the bold made as water (Prov. 28:1). The believer is taught to mark the smote thirty-six, the great disgrace, the hearts melted, the unmanned in sin, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that thirty-six fell and the people's hearts melted like water — the small loss a great rebuke, the courage of Israel broken when God withdrew, the bold made as water in their sin (Prov. 28:1).
6:
"And Joshua rent his clothes, and fell to the earth upon his face before the ark of the LORD until the eventide, he and the elders of Israel, and put dust upon their heads." The witnesses note Joshua and the elders in deep grief, rending their clothes and falling before the ark. The witnesses note that Gill notes the rending of clothes was usual on hearing bad news, expressing grief and trouble. The witnesses note that Henry notes Joshua, as a public person, interested himself more than any in the calamity. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rent his clothes — the token of grief; the fell before the ark — seeking God; the dust on their heads — deep abasement; the humbled before God — under his mighty hand (1 Pet. 5:6).

The witnesses agree the defeat drove Joshua and the elders to humble themselves before God: they "rent their clothes... fell to the earth... before the ark... and put dust upon their heads," the tokens of deep grief and abasement; Henry marks Joshua's public-spirited sorrow, feeling the loss more than any; the turning to God in the place of his presence, prostrate before the ark, shows that the right response to calamity is to humble oneself before him; it teaches that the people of God, when smitten, should seek him in lowliness and prayer, "humble yourselves... under the mighty hand of God" (1 Pet. 5:6), Joshua in the dust before the ark.

So the witnesses make the verse Joshua humbled: The witnesses note the rent his clothes: the token of grief. The witnesses note the fell before the ark: seeking God. The witnesses note the dust on their heads: deep abasement. The witnesses note the humbled before God: under his mighty hand (1 Pet. 5:6). The expositors observe the rent his clothes: the token of grief. The expositors observe the fell before the ark: seeking God. The expositors observe the dust on their heads: deep abasement. The expositors observe the humbled before God: under his mighty hand (1 Pet. 5:6). The believer is taught to mark the rent his clothes, the fell before the ark, the dust on their heads, the humbled before God, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that Joshua and the elders rent their clothes and fell before the ark — humbling themselves before God in the day of calamity, seeking him in lowliness under his mighty hand (1 Pet. 5:6).
7:
"And Joshua said, Alas, O Lord GOD, wherefore hast thou at all brought this people over Jordan, to deliver us into the hand of the Amorites, to destroy us?..." The witnesses note Joshua's anguished cry, questioning why God brought them over Jordan only to be destroyed. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cry — what a distressed condition are we in! who could have thought this would be our case? The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the lament, wherefore hast thou brought us over Jordan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the alas, O Lord GOD — the cry of distress; the wherefore over Jordan — near to the old murmuring; the to destroy us — the despondent complaint; the the saint's weakness — hope thou in God (Ps. 42:11).

The witnesses agree Joshua's lament, though the cry of faith in distress, breathed something of the old murmuring of unbelief: "wherefore hast thou... brought this people over Jordan... to destroy us?" — words near to those of the unbelieving fathers in the wilderness; the shock of defeat wrung from even this good man a complaint that seemed to question God's goodness and purpose; it teaches that the best of saints may, in the heat of trouble, give way to despondent and unbelieving thoughts, yet the cry was still poured out to God; it warns against the murmuring that defeat may provoke, "why art thou cast down, O my soul?... hope thou in God" (Ps. 42:11), Joshua's grief mingled with complaint.

So the witnesses make the verse wherefore over Jordan: The witnesses note the alas, O Lord GOD: the cry of distress. The witnesses note the wherefore over Jordan: near to the old murmuring. The witnesses note the to destroy us: the despondent complaint. The witnesses note the the saint's weakness: hope thou in God (Ps. 42:11). The expositors observe the alas, O Lord GOD: the cry of distress. The expositors observe the wherefore over Jordan: near to the old murmuring. The expositors observe the to destroy us: the despondent complaint. The expositors observe the the saint's weakness: hope thou in God (Ps. 42:11). The believer is taught to mark the alas, O Lord GOD, the wherefore over Jordan, the to destroy us, the the saint's weakness, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that Joshua cried, wherefore hast thou brought us over Jordan to destroy us — the cry of faith mingled with the murmuring of distress, the best of saints giving way to despondent thoughts, yet to be answered by hope in God (Ps. 42:11).
8:
"O Lord, what shall I say, when Israel turneth their backs before their enemies!" The witnesses note Joshua's perplexity — what to say now that Israel has turned its back in defeat. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua knew not what to say for the comfort of Israel, or in vindication of God's power and faithfulness against the charge of weakness or unfaithfulness brought by their enemies. The witnesses note that Wesley sets his being at a loss what to say. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the what shall I say — at a loss; the Israel turns its back — the defeat; the God's honour at stake — the enemy's reproach; the grieved for God's name — what wilt thou do for thy great name (Josh. 7:9).

The witnesses agree Joshua's perplexity arose from the dishonour the defeat seemed to bring on God: "what shall I say, when Israel turneth their backs" — he was at a loss how to answer the reproach of the enemy and to vindicate God's power and faithfulness; the good leader's concern was less for himself than for the LORD's honour, lest the rout give occasion to the heathen to blaspheme; it teaches that the chief grief of the godly in defeat is the reproach it brings on God's name, and the chief care to see that name cleared, "what wilt thou do unto thy great name?" (Josh. 7:9), Joshua troubled for the LORD's honour.

So the witnesses make the verse what shall I say: The witnesses note the what shall I say: at a loss. The witnesses note the Israel turns its back: the defeat. The witnesses note the God's honour at stake: the enemy's reproach. The witnesses note the grieved for God's name: what wilt thou do for thy great name (Josh. 7:9). The expositors observe the what shall I say: at a loss. The expositors observe the Israel turns its back: the defeat. The expositors observe the God's honour at stake: the enemy's reproach. The expositors observe the grieved for God's name: what wilt thou do for thy great name (Josh. 7:9). The believer is taught to mark the what shall I say, the Israel turns its back, the God's honour at stake, the grieved for God's name, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that Joshua was at a loss what to say when Israel turned its back — his grief chiefly for the dishonour to God's name, the godly's care to see the LORD's honour cleared in defeat (Josh. 7:9).
9:
"For the Canaanites and all the inhabitants of the land shall hear of it, and shall environ us round, and cut off our name from the earth: and what wilt thou do unto thy great name?" The witnesses note Joshua's plea grounded on God's glory — the Canaanites would hear and cut off Israel; what then of God's great name? The witnesses note that Gill notes the Canaanites on the east and west who would hear. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the fear they would environ and cut off their name, and the appeal to God's great name. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Canaanites shall hear — and surround Israel; the cut off our name — the threatened destruction; the what wilt thou do — for thy great name; the plead his glory — as Moses pleaded (Exod. 32:12).

The witnesses agree Joshua's prayer rises to its highest and best plea — the glory of God's own name: he fears the Canaanites will hear, surround, and destroy Israel, and asks, "what wilt thou do unto thy great name?"; the binding of Israel's deliverance to God's honour is the strongest argument in prayer, pleading not their worth but his glory; it teaches that the surest ground of intercession is God's own name and glory, that he act for his name's sake, as Moses pleaded before him (Exod. 32:12); Joshua turns his complaint into a plea, that God would not let his great name be reproached among the heathen.

So the witnesses make the verse thy great name: The witnesses note the the Canaanites shall hear: and surround Israel. The witnesses note the cut off our name: the threatened destruction. The witnesses note the what wilt thou do: for thy great name. The witnesses note the plead his glory: as Moses pleaded (Exod. 32:12). The expositors observe the the Canaanites shall hear: and surround Israel. The expositors observe the cut off our name: the threatened destruction. The expositors observe the what wilt thou do: for thy great name. The expositors observe the plead his glory: as Moses pleaded (Exod. 32:12). The believer is taught to mark the the Canaanites shall hear, the cut off our name, the what wilt thou do, the plead his glory, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that Joshua pleaded God's great name, lest the Canaanites cut off Israel — the surest ground of prayer the glory of God's name, that he act for his name's sake (Exod. 32:12).
10:
"And the LORD said unto Joshua, Get thee up; wherefore liest thou thus upon thy face?" The witnesses note God bidding Joshua rise from his prostration — not refusing prayer, but calling him to act. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was said not as refusing his supplication but as encouraging and strengthening him, and to instruct and prepare him for what was to be done. The witnesses note that he was to bestir himself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the get thee up — from his prostration; the wherefore liest thou — not a rebuke of prayer; the a call to act — the sin to be addressed; the prayer issuing in action — wherefore criest thou? go forward (Exod. 14:15).

The witnesses agree God's word "get thee up" was not a rebuke of prayer but a call to action: there was a time to pray and a time to act, and now, the cause of the defeat being sin in the camp, Joshua must rise and put it away; Gill marks it as encouragement and instruction, not refusal; it teaches that prayer must issue in obedience, and that there are seasons when God bids his people leave off lamenting and address the evil that has provoked him, "wherefore criest thou unto me? speak unto the children of Israel, that they go forward" (Exod. 14:15) — Joshua called from his face to his feet.

So the witnesses make the verse get thee up: The witnesses note the get thee up: from his prostration. The witnesses note the wherefore liest thou: not a rebuke of prayer. The witnesses note the a call to act: the sin to be addressed. The witnesses note the prayer issuing in action: wherefore criest thou? go forward (Exod. 14:15). The expositors observe the get thee up: from his prostration. The expositors observe the wherefore liest thou: not a rebuke of prayer. The expositors observe the a call to act: the sin to be addressed. The expositors observe the prayer issuing in action: wherefore criest thou? go forward (Exod. 14:15). The believer is taught to mark the get thee up, the wherefore liest thou, the a call to act, the prayer issuing in action, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that God bade Joshua get up — not refusing prayer but calling him to act, prayer issuing in obedience, the evil in the camp to be addressed, go forward (Exod. 14:15).
11:
"Israel hath sinned, and they have also transgressed my covenant which I commanded them: for they have even taken of the accursed thing, and have also stolen, and dissembled also, and... put it even among their own stuff." The witnesses note God naming the sin — Israel had taken the accursed thing, stolen, dissembled, and hidden it. The witnesses note that Gill notes that though one had committed it, others might have known and connived; there was sin among them, to be discovered and punished. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Israel's sin, transgressing the covenant. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Israel hath sinned — transgressed the covenant; the taken the accursed thing — the theft; the stolen and dissembled — sin upon sin; the nothing hid from God — your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23).

The witnesses agree God's answer lays the cause of the defeat squarely on the sin in the camp, named in its full ugliness: "Israel hath sinned... taken of the accursed thing... stolen... dissembled... put it among their own stuff"; the heaping of the verbs — taken, stolen, dissembled, hidden — shows the manifold wickedness in the one act, a chain of sins growing one from another; it teaches that one sin draws on others, the theft hidden by dissembling, and that nothing is hid from God, "be sure your sin will find you out" (Num. 32:23) — the secret sin laid bare before the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse Israel hath sinned: The witnesses note the Israel hath sinned: transgressed the covenant. The witnesses note the taken the accursed thing: the theft. The witnesses note the stolen and dissembled: sin upon sin. The witnesses note the nothing hid from God: your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23). The expositors observe the Israel hath sinned: transgressed the covenant. The expositors observe the taken the accursed thing: the theft. The expositors observe the stolen and dissembled: sin upon sin. The expositors observe the nothing hid from God: your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23). The believer is taught to mark the Israel hath sinned, the taken the accursed thing, the stolen and dissembled, the nothing hid from God, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that God named the sin — taken, stolen, dissembled, hidden — the manifold wickedness in one act, sin drawing on sin, nothing hid from God, your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23).
12:
"Therefore the children of Israel could not stand before their enemies... because they were accursed: neither will I be with you any more, except ye destroy the accursed from among you." The witnesses note the cause and the cure — Israel could not stand because accursed, nor would God be with them till the accursed thing was destroyed. The witnesses note that Gill notes they could not stand, being forsaken of God for the sin committed among them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the turning of their backs because they were accursed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the could not stand — being accursed; the forsaken of God — the true defeat; the neither will I be with you — till the sin is purged; the the one remedy — purge out the old leaven (1 Cor. 5:7).

The witnesses agree the verse joins the cause of the defeat to its only remedy: they "could not stand... because they were accursed," and God would not be with them again "except ye destroy the accursed"; the withdrawal of God's presence was the true defeat, and the putting away of the sin the one condition of his return; it teaches that unpurged sin forfeits God's favour and that his blessing cannot be restored while the accursed thing remains, the sin must be put away, "purge out therefore the old leaven" (1 Cor. 5:7) — God's presence withheld till the camp be cleansed.

So the witnesses make the verse destroy the accursed: The witnesses note the could not stand: being accursed. The witnesses note the forsaken of God: the true defeat. The witnesses note the neither will I be with you: till the sin is purged. The witnesses note the the one remedy: purge out the old leaven (1 Cor. 5:7). The expositors observe the could not stand: being accursed. The expositors observe the forsaken of God: the true defeat. The expositors observe the neither will I be with you: till the sin is purged. The expositors observe the the one remedy: purge out the old leaven (1 Cor. 5:7). The believer is taught to mark the could not stand, the forsaken of God, the neither will I be with you, the the one remedy, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that Israel could not stand because accursed, nor would God be with them till the accursed thing was destroyed — unpurged sin forfeiting his favour, the blessing not restored till the camp be cleansed, purge out the old leaven (1 Cor. 5:7).
13:
"Up, sanctify the people, and say, Sanctify yourselves against to morrow... there is an accursed thing in the midst of thee, O Israel: thou canst not stand before thine enemies, until ye take away the accursed thing..." The witnesses note the command to sanctify the people and prepare to put away the accursed thing. The witnesses note that Gill notes "up" signifies to bestir himself and be active, first to sanctify the people, giving them orders to do it themselves. The witnesses note that there was an accursed thing in the midst, and they could not stand till it was taken away. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the up, sanctify — bestir and consecrate; the against to morrow — prepared to meet God; the an accursed thing in the midst — the sin to be found; the cleanse the camp — from all filthiness (2 Cor. 7:1).

The witnesses agree the cure begins with sanctification — the people set apart and prepared to deal with the sin: "sanctify the people... there is an accursed thing in the midst... until ye take away the accursed thing"; before the offender could be found and the camp cleansed, the people must consecrate themselves, ready to meet with God and put away the evil; it teaches that the dealing with sin must begin in solemn self-consecration, the church preparing itself to purge out the evil from among it, "let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit" (2 Cor. 7:1), the accursed thing to be taken away that Israel might stand.

So the witnesses make the verse sanctify the people: The witnesses note the up, sanctify: bestir and consecrate. The witnesses note the against to morrow: prepared to meet God. The witnesses note the an accursed thing in the midst: the sin to be found. The witnesses note the cleanse the camp: from all filthiness (2 Cor. 7:1). The expositors observe the up, sanctify: bestir and consecrate. The expositors observe the against to morrow: prepared to meet God. The expositors observe the an accursed thing in the midst: the sin to be found. The expositors observe the cleanse the camp: from all filthiness (2 Cor. 7:1). The believer is taught to mark the up, sanctify, the against to morrow, the an accursed thing in the midst, the cleanse the camp, and to put away the accursed thing. The verse thus records that the people were to sanctify themselves and take away the accursed thing — the dealing with sin beginning in self-consecration, the church preparing to purge out the evil, cleansing from all filthiness (2 Cor. 7:1).
14:
"In the morning therefore ye shall be brought according to your tribes... and the tribe which the LORD taketh shall come according to the families... and the household... man by man." The witnesses note the appointed sifting — tribe by tribe, family by family, man by man, until the guilty be found. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to be brought the next morning before Joshua, the elders, and probably the tabernacle, where they assembled. The witnesses note that the lot would narrow from tribe to family to household to man. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought by tribes — the whole nation sifted; the the tribe the LORD taketh — his own choice; the family and household — narrowing to one; the man by man — no concealment escaping God (Prov. 16:33).

The witnesses agree the manner of discovery — the lot, narrowing from tribe to man — set God himself to find out the hidden offender: "ye shall be brought according to your tribes... man by man," the LORD's own choice singling out the guilty; the gradual sifting magnified the certainty that no concealment could escape him who searches all; it teaches that secret sin cannot hide from God, who has his own means of bringing it to light, "the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Prov. 16:33), the hidden Achan to be picked out from all Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse the sifting by lot: The witnesses note the brought by tribes: the whole nation sifted. The witnesses note the the tribe the LORD taketh: his own choice. The witnesses note the family and household: narrowing to one. The witnesses note the man by man: no concealment escaping God (Prov. 16:33). The expositors observe the brought by tribes: the whole nation sifted. The expositors observe the the tribe the LORD taketh: his own choice. The expositors observe the family and household: narrowing to one. The expositors observe the man by man: no concealment escaping God (Prov. 16:33). The believer is taught to mark the brought by tribes, the the tribe the LORD taketh, the family and household, the man by man, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that the people were to be brought tribe by tribe to man, the LORD singling out the guilty — secret sin unable to hide from God, the disposing of the lot his (Prov. 16:33).
15:
"And it shall be, that he that is taken with the accursed thing shall be burnt with fire... because he hath transgressed the covenant of the LORD, and because he hath wrought folly in Israel." The witnesses note the sentence — the one taken with the accursed thing to be burned, for transgressing the covenant and working folly. The witnesses note that Gill notes burning was one of the four capital punishments with the Jews, ordered here because the accursed city was to be burnt. The witnesses note that he had transgressed the covenant and wrought folly in Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he that is taken — with the accursed thing; the burnt with fire — sharing its doom; the transgressed the covenant — the guilt; the wrought folly — sin a madness (Rom. 6:23).

The witnesses agree the sentence fitted the crime — the taker of the accursed thing to share its doom by fire: "he that is taken... shall be burnt with fire," for "he hath transgressed the covenant... and wrought folly in Israel"; the naming of his sin as covenant-breaking and "folly" marks both its guilt and its senselessness, the trading of the soul for a garment and a wedge of gold; it teaches that sin is folly as well as transgression, a madness against God and self, "the wages of sin is death" (Rom. 6:23), the offender to share the fire of the accursed thing he coveted.

So the witnesses make the verse shall be burnt: The witnesses note the he that is taken: with the accursed thing. The witnesses note the burnt with fire: sharing its doom. The witnesses note the transgressed the covenant: the guilt. The witnesses note the wrought folly: sin a madness (Rom. 6:23). The expositors observe the he that is taken: with the accursed thing. The expositors observe the burnt with fire: sharing its doom. The expositors observe the transgressed the covenant: the guilt. The expositors observe the wrought folly: sin a madness (Rom. 6:23). The believer is taught to mark the he that is taken, the burnt with fire, the transgressed the covenant, the wrought folly, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that the one taken with the accursed thing was to be burned, for transgressing the covenant and working folly — sin both guilt and madness, the wages of sin death (Rom. 6:23).
16:
"So Joshua rose up early in the morning, and brought Israel by their tribes; and the tribe of Judah was taken." The witnesses note Joshua rising early to bring the tribes, and Judah being taken by the lot. The witnesses note that Gill notes his rising early showed his readiness and diligence to obey God's command. The witnesses note that Henry marks the discovery of Achan by the lot, which proved a perfect detection. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rose up early — diligent to obey; the brought the tribes — the sifting begun; the the tribe of Judah — taken first; the a perfect detection — nothing covered but shall be revealed (Matt. 10:26).

The witnesses agree the lot proceeded surely to its mark, Judah taken first: Joshua's early diligence in obeying brought the tribes, and "the tribe of Judah was taken"; Henry notes the lot "proved a perfect detection," God's finger pointing unerringly toward the guilty; the beginning of the sifting shows the relentless certainty of the divine discovery, narrowing step by step; it teaches that God will surely bring the hidden sinner to light, the net of his providence drawing tight, "there is nothing covered that shall not be revealed" (Matt. 10:26), the lot fastening first on Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse Judah taken: The witnesses note the rose up early: diligent to obey. The witnesses note the brought the tribes: the sifting begun. The witnesses note the the tribe of Judah: taken first. The witnesses note the a perfect detection: nothing covered but shall be revealed (Matt. 10:26). The expositors observe the rose up early: diligent to obey. The expositors observe the brought the tribes: the sifting begun. The expositors observe the the tribe of Judah: taken first. The expositors observe the a perfect detection: nothing covered but shall be revealed (Matt. 10:26). The believer is taught to mark the rose up early, the brought the tribes, the the tribe of Judah, the a perfect detection, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that Joshua rose early and Judah was taken by the lot — the relentless certainty of God's discovery, the net drawing tight, nothing covered that shall not be revealed (Matt. 10:26).
17:
"And he brought the family of Judah; and he took the family of the Zarhites: and he brought the family of the Zarhites man by man; and Zabdi was taken." The witnesses note the lot narrowing — the Zarhites taken, then Zabdi. The witnesses note that Gill notes the several families in the tribe, the heads of them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the family of the Zarhites taken, then Zabdi. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the family of Judah — the lot narrowing; the the Zarhites — taken of Judah; the man by man — the circle tightening; the Zabdi taken — secret sins in the light of his face (Ps. 90:8).

The witnesses agree the lot tightened step by step toward the one man: the Zarhites taken of all Judah, and Zabdi of all the Zarhites, the circle drawing ever narrower; the unhurried, certain narrowing magnifies the inescapable hand of God, who can pick one guilty man out of a nation; it teaches the sinner the folly of supposing his hidden sin lost in the crowd, for God can single him out from thousands, "thou hast set... our secret sins in the light of thy countenance" (Ps. 90:8), the lot closing on Zabdi's house.

So the witnesses make the verse Zabdi taken: The witnesses note the the family of Judah: the lot narrowing. The witnesses note the the Zarhites: taken of Judah. The witnesses note the man by man: the circle tightening. The witnesses note the Zabdi taken: secret sins in the light of his face (Ps. 90:8). The expositors observe the the family of Judah: the lot narrowing. The expositors observe the the Zarhites: taken of Judah. The expositors observe the man by man: the circle tightening. The expositors observe the Zabdi taken: secret sins in the light of his face (Ps. 90:8). The believer is taught to mark the the family of Judah, the the Zarhites, the man by man, the Zabdi taken, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that the lot took the Zarhites, then Zabdi — the inescapable hand of God narrowing toward one man, secret sins set in the light of his countenance (Ps. 90:8).
18:
"And he brought his household man by man; and Achan... of the tribe of Judah, was taken." The witnesses note the lot at last fastening on Achan, the guilty man. The witnesses note that Gill notes the household of Zabdi, the heads of each house therein. The witnesses note that Achan was taken. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his household man by man — the last narrowing; the Achan was taken — the guilty found; the singled out — before all Israel; the no concealment availing — spoken in darkness heard in light (Luke 12:3).

The witnesses agree the long sifting ended where God knew from the first — on Achan: "his household man by man; and Achan... was taken," the guilty singled out before all Israel; the unerring lot found the very man who had thought his sin safely hidden; it teaches that no concealment avails against the all-seeing God, who brings the secret offender to open shame, "whatsoever ye have spoken in darkness shall be heard in the light" (Luke 12:3), Achan taken out of all the host, his hidden sin now naked before God and man.

So the witnesses make the verse Achan taken: The witnesses note the his household man by man: the last narrowing. The witnesses note the Achan was taken: the guilty found. The witnesses note the singled out: before all Israel. The witnesses note the no concealment availing: spoken in darkness heard in light (Luke 12:3). The expositors observe the his household man by man: the last narrowing. The expositors observe the Achan was taken: the guilty found. The expositors observe the singled out: before all Israel. The expositors observe the no concealment availing: spoken in darkness heard in light (Luke 12:3). The believer is taught to mark the his household man by man, the Achan was taken, the singled out, the no concealment availing, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that the lot at last took Achan — the guilty singled out, no concealment availing against the all-seeing God, what was hidden now heard in the light (Luke 12:3).
19:
"And Joshua said unto Achan, My son, give, I pray thee, glory to the LORD God of Israel, and make confession unto him; and tell me now what thou hast done; hide it not from me." The witnesses note Joshua tenderly urging Achan to give glory to God and confess his sin. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua treated him in a humane, affectionate, respectable manner, though so great a criminal, being his subject and of the same nation and religion. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Joshua urged him, now detected, to give glory to God by confession. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the my son — the tender address; the give glory to the LORD — by confession; the make confession — owning his sin; the the sinner justifies God — that thou mightest be justified (Ps. 51:4).

The witnesses agree Joshua's dealing with the condemned man was tender and aimed at his soul's good: "my son, give... glory to the LORD... and make confession"; even in judgment he sought Achan's confession, that he might own God's justice and find mercy with him; Clarke marks that confession gives glory to God, the sinner justifying the Judge; it teaches that true repentance owns God's righteousness in its own condemnation, glorifying him by honest confession, "that thou mightest be justified when thou speakest" (Ps. 51:4), Joshua leading even the guilty to glorify God.

So the witnesses make the verse give glory and confess: The witnesses note the my son: the tender address. The witnesses note the give glory to the LORD: by confession. The witnesses note the make confession: owning his sin. The witnesses note the the sinner justifies God: that thou mightest be justified (Ps. 51:4). The expositors observe the my son: the tender address. The expositors observe the give glory to the LORD: by confession. The expositors observe the make confession: owning his sin. The expositors observe the the sinner justifies God: that thou mightest be justified (Ps. 51:4). The believer is taught to mark the my son, the give glory to the LORD, the make confession, the the sinner justifies God, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that Joshua tenderly urged Achan to give glory to God and confess — true repentance owning God's justice in its own condemnation, glorifying him by honest confession (Ps. 51:4).
20:
"And Achan answered Joshua, and said, Indeed I have sinned against the LORD God of Israel, and thus and thus have I done:" The witnesses note Achan's full and open confession of his sin against the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes he made a free and open confession of his sin. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him acknowledging his sin and confessing it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the indeed I have sinned — the frank confession; the against the LORD — owning the true offence; the thus and thus have I done — no excuse or denial; the honest but late — he that confesseth shall have mercy (Prov. 28:13).

The witnesses agree Achan, detected, made a frank and unreserved confession: "Indeed I have sinned against the LORD... and thus and thus have I done"; the owning of the sin as against God, with no excuse or denial, is the mark of honest confession, though it came too late to save his life; it teaches that confession of sin is to be full and free, owning the offence to its true object — God himself; yet it warns that confession compelled by discovery, while right, is not the timely repentance that prevents ruin, "he that confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy" (Prov. 28:13), Achan's confession honest but late.

So the witnesses make the verse I have sinned: The witnesses note the indeed I have sinned: the frank confession. The witnesses note the against the LORD: owning the true offence. The witnesses note the thus and thus have I done: no excuse or denial. The witnesses note the honest but late: he that confesseth shall have mercy (Prov. 28:13). The expositors observe the indeed I have sinned: the frank confession. The expositors observe the against the LORD: owning the true offence. The expositors observe the thus and thus have I done: no excuse or denial. The expositors observe the honest but late: he that confesseth shall have mercy (Prov. 28:13). The believer is taught to mark the indeed I have sinned, the against the LORD, the thus and thus have I done, the honest but late, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that Achan confessed, "indeed I have sinned against the LORD" — full and free confession owning the offence to God, honest yet too late to save his life, he that confesseth and forsaketh shall have mercy (Prov. 28:13).
21:
"When I saw among the spoils a goodly Babylonish garment, and two hundred shekels of silver, and a wedge of gold... then I coveted them, and took them; and, behold, they are hid in the earth in the midst of my tent." The witnesses note Achan tracing his sin — he saw, coveted, took, and hid the spoil. The witnesses note that Gill notes a goodly Babylonish garment, made in Babylon (Shinar), much esteemed; with the silver and the wedge of gold. The witnesses note that he coveted, took, and hid them in his tent. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the when I saw — the eye drew the heart; the I coveted them — the lust within; the and took them — the deed; the hid in my tent — sin conceived bringing death (James 1:14-15).

The witnesses agree Achan's own account lays bare the whole progress of sin — "I saw... I coveted... I took... they are hid": the eye drew the heart, the heart the hand, and the hand the concealment; the four steps trace the deadly course of temptation, from the look to the lust to the deed to the cover-up; it teaches the very anatomy of sin the apostle gives, "when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin... bringeth forth death" (James 1:14-15), and the warning that covetousness, indulged at the eye, ends in ruin, Achan's sin a lesson in the way temptation works.

So the witnesses make the verse saw, coveted, took: The witnesses note the when I saw: the eye drew the heart. The witnesses note the I coveted them: the lust within. The witnesses note the and took them: the deed. The witnesses note the hid in my tent: sin conceived bringing death (James 1:14-15). The expositors observe the when I saw: the eye drew the heart. The expositors observe the I coveted them: the lust within. The expositors observe the and took them: the deed. The expositors observe the hid in my tent: sin conceived bringing death (James 1:14-15). The believer is taught to mark the when I saw, the I coveted them, the and took them, the hid in my tent, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that Achan saw, coveted, took, and hid the spoil — the whole progress of sin from the eye to the deed, lust conceived bringing forth sin and death (James 1:14-15).
22:
"So Joshua sent messengers, and they ran unto the tent; and, behold, it was hid in his tent, and the silver under it." The witnesses note the messengers running to the tent and finding the spoil just as Achan had said. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua sent messengers directly to Achan's tent, to see if it was as he had said and to bring the things. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the haste shown, the messengers running, displaying their zeal. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sent messengers — to the tent; the they ran — in zealous haste; the it was hid — the confession confirmed; the the hidden unearthed — nothing hid but shall be manifested (Mark 4:22).

The witnesses agree the swift errand confirmed the confession to the letter: the messengers "ran unto the tent" and found "it was hid... and the silver under it," exactly as Achan had owned; Calvin marks the haste of the runners as a sign of zeal to vindicate God's justice; the finding of the hidden things proved both Achan's guilt and the truth of the divine discovery; it teaches that the works of darkness are brought to light and proved, the hidden sin unearthed for all to see, "there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested" (Mark 4:22), the buried spoil dug up to confirm the lot.

So the witnesses make the verse the spoil found: The witnesses note the sent messengers: to the tent. The witnesses note the they ran: in zealous haste. The witnesses note the it was hid: the confession confirmed. The witnesses note the the hidden unearthed: nothing hid but shall be manifested (Mark 4:22). The expositors observe the sent messengers: to the tent. The expositors observe the they ran: in zealous haste. The expositors observe the it was hid: the confession confirmed. The expositors observe the the hidden unearthed: nothing hid but shall be manifested (Mark 4:22). The believer is taught to mark the sent messengers, the they ran, the it was hid, the the hidden unearthed, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that the messengers ran and found the spoil hidden as Achan had said — the confession confirmed, the works of darkness brought to light, nothing hid but shall be manifested (Mark 4:22).
23:
"And they took them out of the midst of the tent, and brought them unto Joshua... and laid them out before the LORD." The witnesses note the stolen things taken out and laid before the LORD against whom the sin was done. The witnesses note that Gill notes they took them out of the place, hole, or pit in which they were hid. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them brought to Joshua and all Israel, laid out before the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took them out — from the hiding-place; the brought to Joshua — and all Israel; the laid before the LORD — the offence against him; the exposed before God — all things naked before him (Heb. 4:13).

The witnesses agree the stolen spoil, dug from its hiding-place, was laid "before the LORD" — set openly before him against whom the sin had been committed: the things hidden in the earth were brought into the light, displayed before God and all Israel as the evidence of the crime; the laying of them before the LORD marks that the offence was first against him, and his devoted things restored to his sight; it teaches that what is stolen from God is brought back to his presence in judgment, the secret sin exposed before him from whom it could never be hid, "all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do" (Heb. 4:13).

So the witnesses make the verse laid before the LORD: The witnesses note the took them out: from the hiding-place. The witnesses note the brought to Joshua: and all Israel. The witnesses note the laid before the LORD: the offence against him. The witnesses note the exposed before God: all things naked before him (Heb. 4:13). The expositors observe the took them out: from the hiding-place. The expositors observe the brought to Joshua: and all Israel. The expositors observe the laid before the LORD: the offence against him. The expositors observe the exposed before God: all things naked before him (Heb. 4:13). The believer is taught to mark the took them out, the brought to Joshua, the laid before the LORD, the exposed before God, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that the spoil was laid before the LORD — the offence first against him, the secret sin exposed before God from whom it could never be hid, all things naked and open unto him (Heb. 4:13).
24:
"And Joshua, and all Israel with him, took Achan... and the silver, and the garment, and the wedge of gold, and his sons, and his daughters... and brought them unto the valley of Achor." The witnesses note Achan, the spoil, and his household brought to the valley of Achor for judgment. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua and all Israel are mentioned, to show the agreement between them in the punishment. The witnesses note that Clarke sets Achan and all that was his taken to the valley. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua and all Israel — the nation united; the took Achan and the spoil — the evidence; the his sons and daughters — sharing the guilt; the the valley of Achor — put away the evil (Deut. 13:5).

The witnesses agree the whole nation joined in the solemn execution, bringing Achan, his spoil, and his household to the valley of Achor: "Joshua, and all Israel with him, took Achan... and his sons, and his daughters"; the inclusion of his household, the witnesses note, suggests they shared in the guilt by knowledge or concealment; the gathering of all Israel to the place of judgment shows the public, united putting-away of the sin that had defiled them all; it teaches the seriousness of dealing with the sin that troubles the camp, the whole people concurring to purge the evil, "ye shall put away the evil from among you" (Deut. 13:5), Achan and his brought to Achor.

So the witnesses make the verse brought to Achor: The witnesses note the Joshua and all Israel: the nation united. The witnesses note the took Achan and the spoil: the evidence. The witnesses note the his sons and daughters: sharing the guilt. The witnesses note the the valley of Achor: put away the evil (Deut. 13:5). The expositors observe the Joshua and all Israel: the nation united. The expositors observe the took Achan and the spoil: the evidence. The expositors observe the his sons and daughters: sharing the guilt. The expositors observe the the valley of Achor: put away the evil (Deut. 13:5). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua and all Israel, the took Achan and the spoil, the his sons and daughters, the the valley of Achor, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that Achan, the spoil, and his household were brought to the valley of Achor — the whole nation joining to purge the sin that defiled them, putting away the evil from among them (Deut. 13:5).
25:
"And Joshua said, Why hast thou troubled us? the LORD shall trouble thee this day. And all Israel stoned him with stones, and burned them with fire..." The witnesses note Joshua's word — why hast thou troubled us? the LORD shall trouble thee — and the execution by stoning and fire. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua asked why he had been the occasion of so much trouble by committing this sin. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the LORD troubling him, and all Israel stoning and burning. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the why hast thou troubled us — the troubler of Israel; the the LORD shall trouble thee — requited in kind; the stoned and burned — the evil purged; the mischief recoiling — returning on his own head (Ps. 7:16).

The witnesses agree the sentence answered the sin in kind — the troubler of Israel troubled by the LORD: "why hast thou troubled us? the LORD shall trouble thee this day"; Achan, who had brought trouble on the whole camp, now bore the trouble of God's wrath himself; the execution by stoning and fire purged the evil from Israel; it teaches that the sin which troubles God's people recoils on its author, the LORD requiting the troubler, "his mischief shall return upon his own head" (Ps. 7:16), Achan reaping the trouble he had sown.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD shall trouble thee: The witnesses note the why hast thou troubled us: the troubler of Israel. The witnesses note the the LORD shall trouble thee: requited in kind. The witnesses note the stoned and burned: the evil purged. The witnesses note the mischief recoiling: returning on his own head (Ps. 7:16). The expositors observe the why hast thou troubled us: the troubler of Israel. The expositors observe the the LORD shall trouble thee: requited in kind. The expositors observe the stoned and burned: the evil purged. The expositors observe the mischief recoiling: returning on his own head (Ps. 7:16). The believer is taught to mark the why hast thou troubled us, the the LORD shall trouble thee, the stoned and burned, the mischief recoiling, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that Achan, the troubler of Israel, was troubled by the LORD and executed — the sin recoiling on its author, his mischief returning on his own head (Ps. 7:16).
26:
"And they raised over him a great heap of stones unto this day. So the LORD turned from the fierceness of his anger. Wherefore the name of that place was called, The valley of Achor, unto this day." The witnesses note the heap of stones raised over Achan, God's anger turned away, and the valley named Achor (trouble). The witnesses note that Gill notes the heap of stones raised at the place of his execution as a monument, continuing to the time of writing. The witnesses note that the LORD turned from his anger, and the place was named the valley of Achor. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a heap of stones — the monument of judgment; the the LORD turned — from his fierce anger; the the evil purged — his favour restored; the Achor a door of hope — the valley of trouble turned to mercy (Hos. 2:15).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes with the purging of the sin and the return of God's favour: a heap of stones marked the place, "the LORD turned from the fierceness of his anger," and the valley was named Achor — "trouble" — in memory; the putting-away of the accursed thing reconciled God to his people, his anger ceasing when the evil was removed; it teaches that the removal of sin restores God's favour, and, by a precious gospel turn, the very valley of trouble becomes a place of hope — for the LORD promises to give "the valley of Achor for a door of hope" (Hos. 2:15), the place of judgment turned to mercy, sin purged and anger appeased.

So the witnesses make the verse the anger turned away: The witnesses note the a heap of stones: the monument of judgment. The witnesses note the the LORD turned: from his fierce anger. The witnesses note the the evil purged: his favour restored. The witnesses note the Achor a door of hope: the valley of trouble turned to mercy (Hos. 2:15). The expositors observe the a heap of stones: the monument of judgment. The expositors observe the the LORD turned: from his fierce anger. The expositors observe the the evil purged: his favour restored. The expositors observe the Achor a door of hope: the valley of trouble turned to mercy (Hos. 2:15). The believer is taught to mark the a heap of stones, the the LORD turned, the the evil purged, the Achor a door of hope, and to flee covetousness and confess his sin. The verse thus records that a heap of stones was raised and the LORD turned from his anger, the place named Achor — the purging of sin restoring God's favour, the valley of trouble made a door of hope (Hos. 2:15).
